purpose: Answers: How does fiscal policy sustain or suppress corporate profits and inflation during commodity shocks?
components: Accounting identity: Profits ≡ Investment + Government Deficit + Net Exports − Workers' Savings + Capitalist Consumption
Fiscal channel: large deficits (6-7% GDP) mechanically sustain corporate profits during commodity inflation
Inflation persistence: if fiscal response is accommodative, profits are sustained regardless of monetary policy or consensus positioning
Policy implications: if this mechanism dominates, the key policy variable is fiscal stance, not monetary credibility
Government deficit as corporate profit source (Kalecki identity)
MPC-weighted composition analysis (transfers at 0.7-1.2 vs interest at 0.3-0.5)
Profit floor quantification ($45-65 S&P EPS, 15-35% of total)
Crowding-out interaction under QT (40-50% probability of reactivation)
current_assessment: Channel is real and provides a measurable profit floor. Erosion is mechanically underway as deficit composition shifts toward interest payments. R² of 0.55-0.65 reflects genuine economic variance, not measurement error. The causal direction and crowding-out reactivation under QT are the key uncertainties.
limitations: Direction of causation (deficits → profits vs profits → deficits) is contested
Identity is tautological — the analytical value lies in the causal interpretation which is debatable
Not specifically calibrated for commodity-driven vs other inflationary episodes
Interaction with monetary tightening not fully modeled
R² of 0.55-0.65 means 35-45% of profit variance is unexplained
Causal direction is ambiguous (do deficits cause profits or do profits cause deficits through tax revenue?)
Post-2008 crowding-out attenuation may be QE artifact rather than structural change
Regressions on changes for the corporate sector specifically miss inventory cycles, international profit shifting, and financial vs nonfinancial divergence
